findings.description: yaml.load(f) is called with no Loader, enabling arbitrary Python object construction / code execution from an attacker-controlled config file — the textbook unsafe-YAML hole; must be yaml.safe_load.
Precedence is inverted versus the module docstring: env is applied before the file (lines 67-68), so the config file overwrites environment variables instead of env winning as documented.

get() calls load() which re-opens and re-parses the YAML file and re-scans all of os.environ on every single lookup, so require()/feature_enabled()/repeated get() trigger redundant disk reads and parses with zero caching.

require() rejects on value is None, so a key legitimately set to null cannot satisfy the requirement while it cannot distinguish absent from present-but-None — presence is enforced via a None sentinel collision rather than actual key membership.

Env overlay keys are flat-lowercased scalars and never merged into nested mappings, so APP_FEATURE_FLAGS lands as a coerced string rather than merging into the feature_flags dict, making nested config unreachable via env.

feature_enabled does flags.get(flag) assuming feature_flags is a dict, but if env (or a bad file value) coerces feature_flags to a scalar, .get raises AttributeError instead of returning a clean boolean.

Only file existence is checked; open() and yaml.load can still raise (PermissionError, yaml.YAMLError on malformed input), crashing the loader instead of the graceful empty-dict degradation the missing-file branch promises.
Env key normalization (strip prefix + lowercase) is lossy and non-round-trippable, so distinct env vars can collide on the same normalized key with no namespacing to disambiguate.
